1: 'The word of YHWH was rare (yāqār, H3368) in those days; there was no frequent vision' (ʾên ḥāzôn niphrāṣ). yāqār means precious/rare/scarce — used for precious stones (Proverbs 3:15; Psalms 72:14) and rare events. The scarcity of YHWH's word creates a condition of preciousness-through-absence: rare speech, when it comes, carries extraordinary weight. ḥāzôn (H2377, vision/prophetic revelation) — the technical term for the vision-form of revelation (Isaiah 1:1; Ezekiel 1:1). The paired rarity of divine word and prophetic vision describes a period of spiritual drought that simultaneously marks the darkness just before YHWH breaks his silence — and through the most unexpected medium.
2: Eli's failing eyesight — hēḥēllû ḵĕhôt lōʾ yûḵal lirʾôṯ (had grown so dim he could not see) — is both literal and theologically resonant. The priest who cannot physically see parallels his inability to see the sins of his sons (1 Samuel 2:22-24) and his misreading of Hannah's prayer (1 Samuel 1:12-14). The root kāhāh (H3543, to grow dim/weak) that describes his eyesight is the same root used in 3:13 for his failure to 'restrain' (kāhāh) his sons. His physical dimming and ethical weakness share the same verb root, making the connection explicit. The prophet who 'sees' (receives visions) is coming to replace the priest who cannot see.
3: The lamp of God (nēr ʾĕlōhîm) had not yet gone out — the perpetually burning lampstand of the tabernacle (Exodus 27:20-21; Leviticus 24:2-4) burning from evening to morning. 'Not yet gone out' situates the scene just before dawn: the end of the night watch when the lamp's oil was nearly exhausted. Samuel lies near the ark of God (ʾăšer šām ʾărôn hāʾĕlōhîm) — the presence-locus of YHWH in the sanctuary. Proximity to YHWH's dwelling is the narrative setup for the divine speech: YHWH speaks to the one lying nearest to him. The lamp about to go out + the ark where YHWH dwells = the last moment of the old order, immediately before the new prophetic era begins.
4: YHWH calls 'Samuel! Samuel!' — the doubled name is the formula of urgent divine address: Abraham, Abraham (Genesis 22:11); Moses, Moses (Exodus 3:4); Elijah (1 Kings 19:13); Martha, Martha (Luke 10:41); Saul, Saul (Acts 9:4). The repetition signals a call requiring total attention and complete response — YHWH is summoning a person into a new relationship, not simply conveying information. Samuel's response hinnēnî (H2009, here I am) is the standard formula of covenant availability: Abraham (Genesis 22:1), Moses (Exodus 3:4), Isaiah (Isaiah 6:8). The identical response to YHWH and to Eli (vv5-6) shows Samuel has not yet learned to distinguish the source, but his posture of availability is correct.
5: Samuel runs to Eli thinking he called. Eli's response — 'I did not call; lie down again' (lōʾ qārāʾṯî šûḇ šĕḵāḇ) — is accurate: he genuinely did not call. The pattern of threefold misidentification (vv5-8) before correct identification is the OT's narration of prophetic formation: the call must be recognized before it can be answered rightly. Samuel's assumption that the voice belongs to Eli is natural (who else would call a child in the sanctuary at night?). The narrative's patience with the three errors reflects the understanding that prophetic formation takes time and correction.
6: YHWH calls a second time: 'Samuel!' — and Samuel rises and goes to Eli again. The exact repetition of the call and response sequence with no variation is the narrative's way of showing that the misidentification is genuine, not convenient: Samuel is consistently and sincerely mistaking YHWH's voice for Eli's. The learning process requires the mistake to be repeated until the teacher is able to diagnose the pattern. Eli's second 'I did not call' begins to set up the recognition — he is now hearing the pattern from Samuel's reports that something is happening that is not attributable to him.
7: 'Now Samuel did not yet know YHWH' (wĕšĕmûʾēl ṭerem yāḏaʿ ʾet-YHWH) — the same verb yāḏaʿ as 1 Samuel 2:12 ('Eli's sons did not know YHWH') but with opposite theological valence. Eli's sons did not know YHWH because of corruption; Samuel does not know him because 'the word of YHWH had not yet been revealed to him' (ṭerem yiggāleh ʾēlāyw dĕḇar-YHWH) — YHWH has not yet initiated the relationship from his side. The not-knowing is circumstantial and about to be remedied by divine revelation. The contrast between the two uses of lōʾ yāḏaʿ within a few verses is the narrator's precise theological characterization: same surface, opposite reality.
8: YHWH calls a third time. Eli understands (wayyāḇen, H995 — to discern/perceive; the root of wisdom-discernment, bînāh) that YHWH is calling the boy. This is Eli's last moment of genuine spiritual service: he correctly perceives what is happening and gives Samuel the proper response-formula. The priest who cannot see (v2) and who failed to act against his corrupt sons can still discern a genuine divine call when the pattern makes it undeniable. His instruction is theologically correct: ‘Say, “Speak, YHWH, for your servant hears”’ — he teaches Samuel how to respond to YHWH even though the oracle to follow will pronounce Eli's doom.
9: The formula Eli gives: dabbēr YHWH kî šōmēaʿ ʿaḇdeḵā (Speak, YHWH, for your servant hears). šāmaʿ (H8085, to hear/obey) is the covenant verb — Deuteronomy 6:4 (šĕmaʿ yiśrāʾēl) establishes hearing-that-obeys as the fundamental covenant stance. 'Your servant hears' means 'your servant is in the posture of responsive obedience' — not passive reception but active readiness to respond. The formula models the prophetic stance toward divine speech: total availability, no filtering, responsive action. Samuel will spend his life as a prophet practicing this formula's content.
10: YHWH comes and stands (wayyāḇōʾ YHWH wayyiṯyaṣṣēḇ) — theophanic presence language: YHWH does not merely send a voice but takes up a standing position, indicating a personal appearance (cf. Genesis 18:2; Exodus 34:5; Numbers 22:22). The doubled name formula ('Samuel! Samuel!') repeats the urgency of the initial call. Samuel's response — dabbēr kî šōmēaʿ ʿaḇdeḵā — follows Eli's instruction exactly but omits the divine name: YHWH is present and speaking; the name is not needed for identification. Samuel has learned to address the voice directly.
11: 'Behold, I am about to do something in Israel at which the two ears of everyone who hears it will tingle' (ṣilênāh ʾoznê kol-šōmĕʿô). ṣālan (H6750, to tingle/ring) applied to ears describes news so shocking it creates a physical sensation — used only here, 2 Kings 21:12 (judgment on Manasseh), and Jeremiah 19:3 (judgment on Jerusalem). The formula marks this as one of the most severe judgment pronouncements in the OT. The ear-tingling is the somatic marker of devastating news — the same kind of news that Paul reserves for the severest covenant warnings (Galatians 1:8: 'even if we or an angel from heaven should preach to you a gospel contrary to the one we preached to you, let him be accursed').
12: 'I will fulfill against Eli all that I have spoken concerning his house, from beginning to end' (mēhāḵēl wĕʿaḏ-kallōh). The phrase uses ḵālal (begin) and kālāh (complete/finish): from-beginning-to-end, the full oracle will be fulfilled. This total-fulfillment formula is used for YHWH's covenant commitments (both promises and judgments): what is spoken will be completed without remainder. The word of the man of God (1 Samuel 2:27-36) is now confirmed by YHWH's direct speech — double confirmation by oracle and divine voice establishes the certainty and scope of what is coming.
13: 'I have told him that I am about to punish his house forever, for the iniquity that he knew, because his sons were blaspheming God, and he did not restrain them' (wĕlōʾ kihāh bām). kāhāh (H3543, to restrain/rebuke/dim) — the same root as Eli's dimming eyes. He did not kihah his sons — he did not rebuke with the force of removal from office. The sons' sin: mĕqallĕlîm (H7043, making light/contemptible/cursing) — they made YHWH's name and service contemptible. The combination of knowing the sin (v22: 'Eli heard everything') and not restraining it makes the guilt shared: Eli's passivity becomes complicity in his sons' contempt.
14: 'The iniquity of Eli's house shall not be atoned for by sacrifice or offering forever' (ʾim-yitkappēr ʿăwōn bêṯ-ʿēlî bĕzēḵaḥ ûḇĕminḥāh ʿaḏ-ʿôlām). This is a severe covenant declaration: the two primary sacrificial forms — zāḵaḥ (animal sacrifice, H2077) and minḥāh (grain offering, H4503) — cannot provide atonement for this sin. The system that Hophni and Phinehas corrupted cannot serve as the mechanism of their house's restoration. Numbers 15:30-31 establishes that willful, high-handed rebellion (not unintentional sin) is beyond ordinary sacrificial atonement. The Levitical system's limits are here explicitly stated: some sins require a different remedy — which the rest of Scripture will identify as the sin-bearing that only the sinless can perform.
15: Samuel lay until morning and opened the doors of the sanctuary — his regular morning duty, performed without alteration despite the oracle received. He is afraid to tell the vision (mĕhaggîḏ ʾet-hammārʾeh ʾel-ʿēlî). mārʾeh (H4758, vision/appearance) — the vision received in the night encounter. Samuel's fear is appropriate: the oracle is devastating, and it concerns the man who raised him. The tension between loyalty to Eli and fidelity to YHWH's word is the first test of his prophetic formation. The faithful prophet must report YHWH's word even when it is painful — this test precedes every subsequent oracle Samuel will deliver.
16: Eli calls 'Samuel, my son!' (šĕmûʾēl bĕnî) — the paternal address invokes the relationship. Samuel responds hinnēnî — the same covenant-availability formula he used with YHWH (v4-8): total responsiveness to both YHWH and to those in authority over him. Eli's request: 'Do not hide it from me' (ʾal-nāʾ tĕḵahēd mimmennî) — kāḥaḏ (H3582, to hide/conceal) inverted: tell me everything, conceal nothing. By explicitly requesting full disclosure, Eli creates the covenant obligation for Samuel to report completely — including the devastating personal dimension of the oracle against Eli's own house.
17: Eli adds the self-imprecation oath: 'God do so to you and more also if you hide anything from me' (kōh yāʿăśeh-lĕḵā ʾĕlōhîm wĕḵōh yōsîp̄ ʾim-tĕḵaḥēd mimmennî dāḇār). The standard oath formula (Ruth 3:13; 1 Samuel 14:44; 2 Samuel 3:9) places divine sanctions on Samuel if he withholds anything. Eli has legally bound Samuel to full disclosure. The one who is about to receive the devastating oracle has himself created the obligation for complete reporting — removing any possibility of Samuel softening or omitting elements 'to spare feelings.' Covenant oaths ensure that YHWH's word is transmitted accurately and completely.
18: Samuel reports everything; Eli's response: 'It is YHWH. Let him do what seems good to him' (YHWH hûʾ haṭṭôḇ bĕʿênāyw yaʿăśeh). This is Eli's finest recorded moment and his last significant speech. Submission to YHWH's covenant judgment — without complaint, without self-defense, without seeking to negotiate — is the correct response to a prophetic verdict. The same formula of submission appears in David (2 Samuel 10:12; 1 Samuel 15:26) and characterizes the person who understands that YHWH's judgments, even when devastating, are within YHWH's sovereign goodness. Eli receives the oracle against his house with theological integrity even as he receives it without the practical action that might have changed it.
19: 'YHWH was with Samuel and let none of his words fall to the ground' (wĕlōʾ-hippîl mikkol-dĕḇārāyw ʾārṣāh). The idiom nāpal ʾārṣāh (to fall to the ground) for prophetic words that fail to fulfill — they 'drop' without reaching their target. YHWH's guarantee that none of Samuel's words fall is the Deuteronomic criterion for a true prophet (Deuteronomy 18:21-22: if the word does not come to pass, YHWH did not speak it). Samuel's total reliability as a prophetic channel — established from the first oracle — is the foundation for his national authority. Isaiah 55:11 applies the same principle to YHWH's word itself: 'it shall not return to me empty.'
20: 'All Israel from Dan to Beersheba' (mîḏān wĕʿaḏ-bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ) — the standard expression for the full national extent: Dan in upper Galilee (northern limit) to Beersheba in the Negev (southern limit), approximately 150 miles. 'Knew that Samuel was established (nĕʾĕmān, H539, confirmed/faithful) as a prophet (nāḇîʾ, H5030) of YHWH.' nāḇîʾ — the standard term for prophet applied to Samuel here for the first time. nĕʾĕmān — from ʾāman, the root of 'amen' — characterizes Samuel's prophetic reliability: his word is confirmed, trustworthy, faithful. The nation's recognition of his prophetic authority is the public side of YHWH's establishment of him as the covenant's human mouthpiece.
21: 'And YHWH continued to appear in Shiloh, for YHWH revealed himself to Samuel at Shiloh by the word of YHWH' (wayyōsep̄ YHWH lĕhērāʾôṯ ḇĕšilōh kî niglāh YHWH ʾel-šĕmûʾēl ḇĕšilôh biḏḇar YHWH). Three revelation verbs in one verse: rāʾāh (appear/be seen, H7200), gālāh (reveal/uncover, H1540), dāḇar (speak/word, H1696). The triple characterization — appearing, revealing, speaking — describes the full range of divine self-disclosure. After v1's rare word and absent vision, YHWH now regularly appears at Shiloh through Samuel. The new prophetic era has begun: the drought of divine speech is over; YHWH's word comes now through his established prophet.
